source: Stull chart, Digitalfire, Glazy, Ceramic Arts Network

low_fire.notes: Low-fire glazes (cone 06-3) need more flux to mature. They appear glossier at lower SiO2:Al2O3 ratios due to higher flux load and lower melting point. High lead or boron content common.

mid_range.notes: Mid-range glazes (cone 4-6) are transitional. More boron is typically used than high-fire. SiO2:Al2O3 ratios are slightly lower than cone 10 for equivalent surfaces.

high_fire.notes: High-fire glazes (cone 8-11). Stull chart original context (cone 11, 0.3 R2O:0.7 RO). These are the classic thresholds.

notes: Based on Stull chart (1912) as implemented on Glazy.org. Matte: ratio < threshold, Semi-matte (satin): ratio ~threshold-range, Glossy: ratio > threshold. Thresholds vary by cone because lower temperatures require more flux for the same melt. High-flux glazes (CaO, B2O3) may be glossy at lower ratios. These thresholds are guidance, not rules.
caveats: High CaO can promote gloss at lower SiO2:Al2O3 ratios
High MgO can promote matte at higher SiO2:Al2O3 ratios
B2O3 increases fluidity, potentially lowering effective matte threshold
Underfired glazes may appear matte regardless of ratio
Cooling rate affects crystal formation and surface development
Lead glazes (low-fire) can be glossy at very low SiO2:Al2O3 ratios
These ratios are guidelines for formulation guidance, not absolute rules
